RC.RP-01.title: Recovery Plan Execution
RC.RP-01.assessorFocus: The assessor verifies that the organization executes the recovery portion of its incident response plan once initiated, including that all individuals with recovery responsibilities are aware of plans and authorizations required.

evidenceItems.name: Incident Recovery Plan
evidenceItems.description: Documents the recovery plan that defines restoration procedures, roles, responsibilities, and authorization requirements for recovery activities.
whatGoodLooksLike: Recovery plan is integrated with or cross-referenced from the incident response plan
Roles and responsibilities for recovery activities are clearly defined with named individuals
Authorization requirements for each recovery action are specified
Plan includes prioritized recovery sequences aligned with the BIA
commonGaps: No formal recovery plan exists; recovery is improvised
Recovery roles and responsibilities are undefined or assigned to unavailable personnel
Authorization requirements for recovery actions are not specified
Recovery sequencing does not align with business impact analysis
suggestedSources: Business continuity plan / disaster recovery plan

evidenceItems.name: Recovery Activation and Execution Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that recovery plans were activated and executed during actual incidents with documented actions, timelines, and responsible parties.
whatGoodLooksLike: Recovery activation is formally documented with timestamp and authorizing party
Execution steps are logged with responsible individuals and completion status
Recovery timelines are tracked against RTO/RPO objectives
Deviations from the plan are documented with justification
commonGaps: No formal activation record for recovery activities
Recovery steps are not logged or tracked against the plan
RTO/RPO performance is not measured during recovery
Plan deviations are not documented or justified
suggestedSources: IR case management tool (TheHive, IBM Resilient)
DR orchestration tool (Zerto, Veeam DR Orchestrator)

evidenceItems.name: Recovery Personnel Notification and Acknowledgment Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that all individuals with recovery responsibilities are notified of recovery plan activation and acknowledge their assignments.
whatGoodLooksLike: Notification records show all recovery team members were contacted
Acknowledgment of recovery assignments is documented
Backup personnel are notified when primary assignees are unavailable
Notification is automated through mass communication or on-call systems
commonGaps: Recovery personnel are not formally notified; they learn informally
No acknowledgment tracking for recovery assignments
Contact lists for recovery personnel are outdated
No backup personnel identified for critical recovery roles

assessmentTips: Request recovery activation records from the most recent incident requiring recovery
Verify that recovery team contact lists are current and tested periodically
Check that RTO/RPO objectives are defined and that recovery execution is measured against them
Confirm that authorization requirements for recovery actions are followed during actual incidents
Review whether recovery plan deviations are documented and feed into plan improvements

RC.RP-02.title: Recovery Action Selection and Prioritization
RC.RP-02.assessorFocus: The assessor examines whether recovery actions are selected, scoped, and prioritized based on defined criteria and available resources, and whether plans are adapted when organizational needs or resource availability changes.

evidenceItems.name: Recovery Action Selection Criteria and Framework
evidenceItems.description: Documents the criteria used to select and prioritize recovery actions based on business impact, available resources, and incident characteristics.
whatGoodLooksLike: Selection criteria reference BIA-derived recovery priorities (RTO, RPO, MTPD)
Framework accounts for resource constraints and dependencies
Criteria enable dynamic re-prioritization as conditions change
Framework is tested during DR exercises
commonGaps: No defined criteria for selecting recovery actions
Recovery prioritization does not reference BIA data
Framework is rigid and does not account for resource constraints
Criteria have not been validated through exercises
suggestedSources: Business continuity plan / disaster recovery plan

evidenceItems.name: Recovery Action Execution and Reprioritization Logs
evidenceItems.description: Shows that recovery actions were selected, prioritized, and adapted during actual incidents based on evolving conditions.
whatGoodLooksLike: Recovery action logs show prioritization aligned with BIA and available resources
Re-prioritization events are documented with justification
Resource allocation decisions are logged
Recovery progress is tracked against prioritized action plans
commonGaps: Recovery actions are not prioritized; all actions attempted simultaneously
Re-prioritization decisions are not documented
Resource constraints are not considered in recovery sequencing
No tracking of recovery progress against the prioritized plan
suggestedSources: DR orchestration tool (Zerto, Veeam DR Orchestrator)

evidenceItems.name: DR Exercise Recovery Prioritization Test Results
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that recovery action selection and prioritization procedures are validated through disaster recovery exercises.
whatGoodLooksLike: DR exercises test recovery prioritization under resource-constrained scenarios
Exercise results validate that BIA-driven priorities are achievable
Lessons learned from exercises drive updates to prioritization criteria
Exercises include scenarios requiring dynamic re-prioritization
commonGaps: DR exercises do not test recovery prioritization
Exercises assume unlimited resources and do not test constraints
Exercise findings do not lead to updates in recovery criteria
No scenarios test the need to re-prioritize during recovery

assessmentTips: Verify that recovery prioritization criteria reference BIA-derived RTO and RPO values
Ask for an example of when recovery priorities were changed during an incident and review the documentation
Check that DR exercises include scenarios with resource constraints requiring prioritization trade-offs
Confirm that recovery action selection considers dependencies between systems
Review whether recovery progress tracking tools provide real-time visibility to incident commanders

RC.RP-03.title: Backup and Restoration Asset Integrity Verification
RC.RP-03.assessorFocus: The assessor verifies that the organization checks the integrity of backups and other restoration assets for indicators of compromise, file corruption, and other integrity issues before using them for restoration.

evidenceItems.name: Backup Integrity Verification Procedures
evidenceItems.description: Documents the procedures for verifying backup integrity before using backups for incident recovery, including checks for compromise and corruption.
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedures mandate IOC scanning of backup data before restoration
File integrity checks (checksums, hash verification) are required before use
Procedures address verification of different backup types (full, incremental, snapshot)
Clean backup identification procedures account for the incident timeline
commonGaps: No procedures for verifying backup integrity before restoration
Backups are restored without checking for IOCs from the incident
File integrity checks are not performed or not documented
No consideration of whether backups predate the compromise

evidenceItems.name: Backup Integrity Verification Logs
evidenceItems.description: Shows that backup integrity was verified during actual recovery operations, including IOC scans and integrity checks.
whatGoodLooksLike: Verification logs show IOC scans were run against backup data before restoration
Hash verification confirms backup file integrity
Verification results are documented and approved before restoration proceeds
Failed verifications are investigated before alternative backups are used
commonGaps: No verification logs exist for backup integrity during recovery
IOC scanning of backups is skipped due to time pressure
Failed integrity checks are ignored rather than investigated
Verification is performed inconsistently across different recovery operations
suggestedSources: EDR platform scan results on restored data

evidenceItems.name: Routine Backup Verification Test Results
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that backups are regularly tested for recoverability and integrity as part of ongoing operations, not just during incidents.
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular backup restoration tests are performed (at least quarterly)
Test results document successful data recovery and integrity confirmation
Failed backup tests are tracked as incidents with remediation
Test coverage includes all critical systems identified in the BIA
commonGaps: Backup restoration tests are not performed regularly
Tests only cover a small subset of backed-up systems
Failed tests are not tracked or remediated
Test results are not documented or reported to management
suggestedSources: IT operations testing schedules and results

evidenceItems.name: Immutable Backup Configuration Evidence
evidenceItems.description: Shows that backup infrastructure includes immutability features to protect against ransomware and insider threats compromising backup data.
whatGoodLooksLike: Immutable backup storage is configured with retention locks
Air-gapped or offline backup copies exist for critical systems
Backup infrastructure access is restricted and monitored
Immutability settings are audited regularly
commonGaps: No immutable backup capabilities are configured
Air-gapped backups do not exist for critical systems
Backup infrastructure has excessive administrative access
Immutability settings have not been validated or audited
suggestedSources: Backup console configuration (Veeam, Commvault, Cohesity, Rubrik)
Cloud storage immutability settings (AWS S3 Object Lock, Azure Immutable Blob)

assessmentTips: Request backup restoration test results from the last quarter and verify coverage of critical systems
Verify that IOC scanning of backups is documented in recent recovery operations
Check that immutable backup storage is configured and that immutability cannot be overridden by a single administrator
Confirm that backup integrity verification procedures account for the incident compromise timeline
Ask how the organization determines which backup is the most recent clean copy during a ransomware incident

RC.RP-04.title: Post-Incident Operational Norms
RC.RP-04.assessorFocus: The assessor verifies that the organization considers critical mission functions and cybersecurity risk management when establishing post-incident operational norms, ensuring essential services are restored in the correct order and validated by system owners.

evidenceItems.name: Service Restoration Priority and Sequencing Documentation
evidenceItems.description: Defines the order in which services and systems are restored based on BIA data and service delivery objectives.
whatGoodLooksLike: Restoration sequence aligns with BIA-defined RTOs and critical mission functions
Dependencies between systems are mapped and considered in sequencing
Service delivery objectives (SDOs) define acceptable post-recovery performance levels
Restoration priorities are reviewed and updated when BIA is refreshed
commonGaps: No defined restoration sequence; systems are restored ad hoc
BIA data does not inform restoration priorities
System dependencies are not mapped, causing restoration failures
Restoration priorities are outdated and do not reflect current business needs

evidenceItems.name: System Owner Restoration Confirmation Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that system owners confirm successful restoration and return to normal operations for their systems.
whatGoodLooksLike: System owners sign off on restoration completion for each recovered system
Confirmation includes validation of data integrity, functionality, and performance
Confirmation records are linked to the incident and recovery records
Unresolved issues identified during confirmation are tracked to resolution
commonGaps: No system owner sign-off on restoration completion
Restoration is declared complete by IT without business validation
Post-restoration issues are not tracked or linked to the incident
System owners are not involved in verifying restoration adequacy

evidenceItems.name: Post-Recovery Performance Monitoring Reports
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that restored systems are monitored after recovery to verify they meet performance baselines and operate normally.
whatGoodLooksLike: Enhanced monitoring is applied to restored systems for a defined post-recovery period
Performance baselines are compared against pre-incident and post-recovery metrics
Anomalies detected during post-recovery monitoring are investigated
Post-recovery monitoring period and criteria are defined in the recovery plan
commonGaps: No enhanced monitoring of restored systems post-recovery
Performance baselines are not available for comparison
Post-recovery monitoring period is not defined
Anomalies during post-recovery are not investigated as potential re-compromise
suggestedSources: Infrastructure monitoring platform (Datadog, Dynatrace, Zabbix, Nagios)

assessmentTips: Verify that restoration sequencing aligns with BIA-defined RTOs and critical mission functions
Request system owner sign-off records from the most recent recovery operation
Check that post-recovery monitoring is enhanced for a defined period after restoration
Confirm that system dependencies are mapped and considered in the restoration sequence
Ask how the organization determines when post-incident operations return to normal baseline

RC.RP-05.title: Restored Asset Integrity Verification and Service Restoration
RC.RP-05.assessorFocus: The assessor examines whether the organization verifies the integrity of restored assets, confirms root cause remediation, and validates that systems and services operate correctly before returning them to production.

evidenceItems.name: Post-Restoration IOC and Integrity Verification Reports
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that restored assets are scanned for indicators of compromise and verified for integrity before being returned to production.
whatGoodLooksLike: IOC scans are performed on all restored systems before production use
Root cause remediation is verified (e.g., vulnerability patched, access closed)
Integrity checks confirm system configurations match approved baselines
Verification results are approved by the incident commander before go-live
commonGaps: Restored systems are returned to production without IOC scanning
Root cause remediation is not verified before restoration
Configuration integrity is not checked against baselines
No approval gate between verification and production deployment
suggestedSources: EDR platform (CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint)

evidenceItems.name: Service Restoration Validation Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that restored services are tested for correct functionality before full production use is resumed.
whatGoodLooksLike: Functional testing is performed on restored services before production cutover
Test results document that all critical functions operate correctly
Data integrity verification confirms no data loss or corruption
User acceptance testing (UAT) is performed for customer-facing services
commonGaps: No functional testing of restored services before production use
Data integrity is not verified after restoration
Testing is limited to basic connectivity checks
No user acceptance testing for restored customer-facing services

evidenceItems.name: Normal Operating Status Confirmation Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that normal operating status is formally confirmed for each restored system and service.
whatGoodLooksLike: Formal go/no-go decision is documented for each system return to production
Normal operating status criteria are predefined and evaluated
Confirmation involves both IT and business stakeholders
Confirmation records are linked to the incident and recovery records
commonGaps: No formal confirmation of normal operating status
Normal operating status criteria are not predefined
IT declares systems restored without business stakeholder confirmation
No linkage between restoration confirmation and incident records

assessmentTips: Review post-restoration verification reports from a recent incident to confirm IOC scanning was performed
Verify that root cause remediation is confirmed before systems return to production
Check that functional testing goes beyond basic connectivity to verify critical service operations
Confirm that a formal go/no-go decision process exists for returning restored systems to production
Ask whether configuration baselines are used to verify restored systems match approved states

RC.RP-06.title: Recovery Closure and Lessons Learned
RC.RP-06.assessorFocus: The assessor verifies that incident recovery is formally closed based on defined criteria, and that comprehensive after-action reports documenting the incident, response/recovery actions, and lessons learned are prepared and retained.

evidenceItems.name: Recovery Closure Criteria and Declaration Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that recovery closure criteria are defined and formally applied to declare the end of incident recovery.
whatGoodLooksLike: Closure criteria include system restoration confirmation, monitoring results, and stakeholder sign-off
Formal closure declaration is documented with timestamp and authorizing party
Closure is not declared until all recovery actions are verified complete
Outstanding items from recovery are transitioned to normal operations tracking
commonGaps: No defined criteria for declaring recovery complete
Recovery ends informally without formal closure declaration
Closure is declared while recovery actions remain incomplete
Outstanding items are not transitioned to operational tracking
suggestedSources: IR case management tool (TheHive, IBM Resilient)

evidenceItems.name: After-Action Reports (AARs)
evidenceItems.description: Documents the comprehensive after-action report prepared for each significant incident, covering the incident itself, response and recovery actions, and lessons learned.
whatGoodLooksLike: AAR covers incident timeline, root cause, response actions, recovery actions, and outcomes
Lessons learned are specific, actionable, and assigned to owners with deadlines
AAR is reviewed by all participating teams and approved by incident commander
AAR completion SLA is defined and tracked (e.g., within 2 weeks of closure)
commonGaps: After-action reports are not prepared for most incidents
AARs are superficial and do not include actionable lessons learned
Lessons learned are documented but not assigned owners or tracked to completion
AARs are delayed significantly or never completed after incidents
suggestedSources: IR case management tool post-incident module

evidenceItems.name: Lessons Learned Tracking and Implementation Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that lessons learned from incidents are tracked as corrective actions through to implementation and that they drive improvements to policies, procedures, and controls.
whatGoodLooksLike: Lessons learned are logged as corrective actions in a tracking system
Each action has an owner, deadline, and status
Completed actions are verified for effectiveness
Patterns across multiple incidents are identified and addressed systemically
commonGaps: Lessons learned are documented but never acted upon
No tracking system for corrective actions from incidents
Actions lack owners or deadlines and languish indefinitely
Same lessons appear repeatedly because prior actions were not effective
suggestedSources: Risk register updates from lessons learned

evidenceItems.name: Post-Incident Review Meeting Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that structured post-incident review meetings are held to discuss the incident, evaluate the response and recovery, and identify improvements.
whatGoodLooksLike: Post-incident reviews are conducted within a defined timeframe after closure
Meetings include representatives from all teams involved in the response and recovery
Meeting minutes capture discussion points, decisions, and assigned actions
Reviews use a blameless retrospective approach to encourage candid participation
commonGaps: Post-incident reviews are not conducted
Reviews only include the security team, excluding IT and business stakeholders
Meeting minutes are not captured or retained
Reviews are blame-focused, discouraging honest feedback

assessmentTips: Request after-action reports from the last three significant incidents and verify completeness
Trace lessons learned from a specific incident to verify they were tracked to implementation
Check that post-incident reviews include cross-functional participation beyond the security team
Verify that recovery closure criteria exist and were formally applied in recent incidents
Ask whether the organization identifies patterns across multiple incident AARs for systemic improvement

RC.CO-01.title: Withdrawn - Incorporated into RC.CO-04
RC.CO-01.assessorFocus: This subcategory has been withdrawn in NIST CSF 2.0 and incorporated into RC.CO-04. Assessors should evaluate the relevant controls under RC.CO-04 (Public Updates on Incident Recovery).

evidenceItems.name: Refer to RC.CO-04 Evidence
evidenceItems.description: All evidence requirements for this withdrawn subcategory are now addressed under RC.CO-04.
whatGoodLooksLike: Organization maps legacy RC.CO-01 controls to RC.CO-04
Public communication procedures cover original RC.CO-01 scope
No orphaned controls from the withdrawal
commonGaps: Organization still references RC.CO-01 without mapping to RC.CO-04
Legacy procedures tied to RC.CO-01 have not been migrated
Control mapping documentation is incomplete

assessmentTips: Verify that controls previously mapped to RC.CO-01 have been remapped to RC.CO-04
Ensure no evidence gaps exist from the subcategory withdrawal
Review the organization's CSF version migration documentation

RC.CO-02.title: Withdrawn - Incorporated into RC.CO-04
RC.CO-02.assessorFocus: This subcategory has been withdrawn in NIST CSF 2.0 and incorporated into RC.CO-04. Assessors should evaluate the relevant controls under RC.CO-04 (Public Updates on Incident Recovery).

evidenceItems.name: Refer to RC.CO-04 Evidence
evidenceItems.description: All evidence requirements for this withdrawn subcategory are now addressed under RC.CO-04.
whatGoodLooksLike: Organization maps legacy RC.CO-02 controls to RC.CO-04
Reputation recovery procedures cover original RC.CO-02 scope
No orphaned controls from the withdrawal
commonGaps: Organization still references RC.CO-02 without mapping to RC.CO-04
Legacy procedures tied to RC.CO-02 have not been migrated
Control mapping documentation is incomplete

assessmentTips: Verify that controls previously mapped to RC.CO-02 have been remapped to RC.CO-04
Ensure no evidence gaps exist from the subcategory withdrawal
Review the organization's CSF version migration documentation

RC.CO-03.title: Internal and External Recovery Communication
RC.CO-03.assessorFocus: The assessor examines whether the organization communicates recovery activities and progress to designated internal and external stakeholders in a secure, consistent manner aligned with response plans and contractual obligations.

evidenceItems.name: Recovery Communication Plan and Templates
evidenceItems.description: Documents the communication plan for recovery activities including stakeholder lists, communication channels, frequency, and pre-approved message templates.
whatGoodLooksLike: Communication plan defines stakeholder groups, channels, and update frequencies
Pre-approved message templates exist for common recovery scenarios
Secure communication channels are specified for sensitive recovery information
Communication responsibilities are assigned to named individuals
commonGaps: No formal communication plan for recovery activities
No pre-approved templates; messages are drafted ad hoc during recovery
Communication channels are not secure or appropriate for sensitive content
Communication responsibilities are undefined

evidenceItems.name: Recovery Status Update Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that recovery progress updates were provided to designated stakeholders at defined intervals during actual incidents.
whatGoodLooksLike: Regular status updates to senior leadership are documented with timestamps
Updates include recovery progress, estimated completion, and outstanding risks
Stakeholder acknowledgment of status updates is captured
Update frequency increases for high-severity incidents
commonGaps: Recovery status updates are informal and not documented
Senior leadership receives inconsistent or infrequent updates
Updates lack estimated completion times or outstanding risk information
No escalation when recovery falls behind schedule
suggestedSources: IR case management tool communication logs
Incident bridge call notes or recordings

evidenceItems.name: Supplier and Partner Recovery Communication Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates that recovery information is shared with critical suppliers and partners in accordance with contractual obligations.
whatGoodLooksLike: Communication with suppliers follows contractually defined protocols
Recovery progress affecting shared services or supply chain is communicated promptly
Crisis communication with critical suppliers uses pre-established channels
Supplier input on recovery priorities is solicited when their services are affected
commonGaps: Suppliers are not informed of recovery activities that affect shared services
Contractual communication obligations are not followed during recovery
No pre-established communication channels with critical suppliers
Supplier impact is not considered in recovery communication planning
suggestedSources: Email and secure messaging archives with suppliers

assessmentTips: Request recovery communication records from the most recent significant incident
Verify that senior leadership received regular, documented updates during recovery
Check that communication templates exist and were used during actual recovery events
Confirm that supplier communication follows contractual obligations
Review whether secure communication channels are specified and used for sensitive recovery information

RC.CO-04.title: Public Incident Recovery Updates
RC.CO-04.assessorFocus: The assessor examines whether the organization has approved methods and messaging for public communications about incident recovery, including compliance with breach notification requirements and communication of preventive measures.

evidenceItems.name: Public Communication Procedures and Approval Workflows
evidenceItems.description: Documents the procedures for issuing public statements about incident recovery, including approval chains and authorized spokespersons.
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedures define who can authorize and deliver public communications
Approval workflow includes legal, executive, and communications team review
Messaging guidelines ensure consistency and accuracy
Procedures address social media, press releases, and website notifications
commonGaps: No formal approval process for public incident communications
Authorized spokespersons are not designated
Legal review is not required before public statements
Social media communications are not included in the procedures

evidenceItems.name: Public Communication Records and Notifications
evidenceItems.description: Shows actual public communications issued during incident recovery, including breach notifications, press statements, and customer communications.
whatGoodLooksLike: All public communications are archived with approval records
Breach notification letters comply with regulatory requirements
Communications explain recovery steps and preventive measures being taken
Customer-facing FAQ documents address common concerns
commonGaps: Public communications are not archived
Breach notifications do not comply with all applicable regulatory requirements
Communications do not explain what the organization is doing to prevent recurrence
No proactive communication; organization only responds to media inquiries

evidenceItems.name: Regulatory Breach Notification Compliance Records
evidenceItems.description: Demonstrates compliance with mandatory breach notification requirements including filing confirmations and timeline adherence.
whatGoodLooksLike: Filing confirmations from regulatory bodies are retained
Notification timelines demonstrate compliance with jurisdictional requirements
Notification content meets regulatory data element requirements
Legal counsel review of notifications is documented
commonGaps: No filing confirmations are retained
Notification timelines exceed regulatory deadlines
Notification content is incomplete per regulatory requirements
Legal counsel was not involved in notification preparation

evidenceItems.name: Crisis Communication Exercise and Preparedness Records
evidenceItems.description: Shows that the organization tests its public communication procedures through exercises to ensure readiness.
whatGoodLooksLike: Crisis communication is included in tabletop exercises
Mock press conferences or media responses are practiced
Pre-approved holding statements are prepared and accessible
Spokesperson media training is documented
commonGaps: Public communication is not tested during incident exercises
No pre-approved holding statements exist
Designated spokespersons have not received media training
Crisis communication preparedness is not assessed
suggestedSources: Tabletop exercise reports including communication scenarios
Media training records for designated spokespersons

assessmentTips: Request copies of public communications issued during the most recent publicly disclosed incident
Verify that approval workflows for public communications include legal review
Check that pre-approved holding statements exist and are accessible during incidents
Confirm that designated spokespersons have received media training
Review regulatory breach notification filings for timeline compliance and completeness
